Title: An alternative splicing caused by a natural variation in BnaC02.VTE4 gene affects vitamin E and glucosinolate content in rapeseed (Brassica napus L.)

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: terminal methylation step of tocopherol biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Methylates gamma-tocopherol to form alpha-tocopherol using SAM as the methyl donor.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as rate-limiting/final-step enzyme for alpha-tocopherol biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Vitamin E is an essential dietary antioxidant and beneficial nutrient; reduced glucosinolate improves rapeseed seed quality and decreases harmful anti-nutritional compounds.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: mature seeds; developing seeds (4–6 weeks after pollination); also detected in leaves and pod coats
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Functional HapH allele promotes vitamin E and alpha-tocopherol accumulation and reduces glucosinolate content; HapL loss-of-function allele lowers alpha-tocopherol and increases glucosinolate.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GWAS identified qVE.C02 for vitamin E and alpha-tocopherol, and BnaC02.VTE4 was validated as the target gene by complementation. 2. A natural A-to-G mutation at the 3' splice site of intron 2 in HapL caused alternative splicing, premature translation termination, and loss of part of the Ubie_methyltran domain. 3. Full-length HapH, but not HapL, restored vitamin E/alpha-tocopherol in Arabidopsis atvte4-4 and increased these traits in low-VE rapeseed lines. 4. Site-directed reversion HapL1052(G-A) rescued vitamin E and alpha-tocopherol accumulation, proving the causal nucleotide. 5. Multi-omics and phenotyping showed HapH is associated with higher vitamin E and lower glucosinolate, whereas HapL is associated with activation of glucosinolate biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Expressed in multiple tissues; highest in flower, followed by flower bud; in developing seeds expression is higher in HapH than HapL and peaks in the critical 4–6 week seed filling period, with the largest difference at 6 W.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: In Brassica napus, expressed across leaf, stem, bud, flower, seed and pod coat; strongest metabolic effect observed in developing seeds.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: GWAS; F2 genotype-phenotype analysis; Arabidopsis mutant complementation; rapeseed complementation; transcript cloning; site-directed mutagenesis; qRT-PCR; RNA-seq; metabolomics; metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Field growth across 2019 and 2020; seed developmental stages 4 W and 6 W after pollination were critical for interpretation.
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Native genomic fragment including 2.2 kb upstream promoter for full-length complementation; CaMV 35S constitutive promoter for transcript overexpression
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: A favorable natural allele/HapH and derived KASP marker C02Vte4 can be used for marker-assisted selection to increase seed vitamin E/alpha-tocopherol and simultaneously reduce glucosinolate; both hybrid parents should be improved because embryo genotype predominates for seed vitamin E. Editing the causal splice-site nucleotide in HapL is a direct engineering strategy.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Paper suggests favorable HapH may also improve fatty acid traits and possibly stress resistance, but direct agronomic penalties were not established.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: HapL carries a point mutation at 1052 bp (A-to-G at the 3' splice site of intron 2), generating transcripts HapL-T1 and HapL-T2; both undergo premature termination and lose 24 aa or 41 aa in the Ubie_methyltran domain. HapH was widely selected during domestication, especially in semi-winter and winter ecotypes. The paper proposes BnaC02.VTE4 affects metabolic flux of tyrosine and methionine/SAM, thereby linking tocopherol and glucosinolate biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: RNA-seq and non-target metabolomics; National Genomics Data Center GSA Bioproject PRJCA032597
